title: Fancy X Farmhouse Bench
summary: A dining bench on three decorative X-braced legs built from beveled 2x2 crosspieces — exact cut lists given for both a 63" and a 96" length, since only the top and stretcher change between the two.
description:
The X in each leg is built from short and long 2x2 pieces beveled at 45° on both ends, capped top and bottom with 1x2 trim — build one leg completely first and use it as your physical pattern for the remaining two, rather than trying to work from measurements alone for each one. **Three legs support this bench, not two** — an end leg at each side plus a center leg, which is what lets the design work at both the 63" and 96" lengths without the seat boards sagging.

Attach the 2x6 seat boards to the leg tops with pocket hole screws and glue, then add the decorative bottom stretcher centered between the legs — purely decorative on the shorter bench, but the plan suggests optional cross braces running from the center support out to each leg on the longer 96" version for extra rigidity. **Only the top boards and the stretcher change length between the two sizes** — every leg part is identical whichever length you build, which is why both complete cut lists are given here rather than one size with a scaling note.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Long point to long point, both ends cut at 45° off square, ends not parallel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 45° off square, ends not parallel.
Puts the bench legs just outside the table legs so the bench can slide in.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Decide which bench you are building first, because only the seat boards and the stretcher trim change between the two — every leg part is identical either way. From the three 8 ft 2x2s, cut twelve short X pieces 6" and eight long X pieces 16 1/2", each measured long point to long point with both ends at 45° off square and the two ends NOT parallel. From the 3 ft 2x4, cut three leg center posts 11 1/4". From the two 8 ft 1x2s, cut six cap trim pieces 13 1/2" and six cap trim pieces 2 1/2". From the 8 ft 1x3, cut two stretcher trim pieces — 26 1/4" for the 63" bench or 34 1/2" for the 96" one. Then cut three 2x6 seat boards to 63" or 96". If you are building the 96" bench, note that an 8 ft 2x6 is nominally 96": the seat boards use the entire board with nothing left to square the factory ends, so buy longer stock if you want clean ends.

steps.title: Build the legs
steps.body: Build one leg completely and use it as the physical pattern for the other two rather than re-measuring each. Each leg is the 11 1/4" 2x4 center post with the mitered 2x2 X pieces radiating off it and the 1x2 cap trim closing top and bottom. Work on a flat surface over a drawn layout line so both halves of the X sit in one plane; a 45° miter that is a degree off shows as a gap at the joint. Glue every mitered face, because end grain at 45° gives a screw very little to hold, and clamp until set. The twelve short X pieces divide evenly at four per leg, but the eight long ones do not divide by three — check the count against your first finished leg before you commit the rest.

steps.title: Attach the stretchers
steps.body: Stand the three legs upright and parallel, one at each end and one in the middle, and run the 1x3 stretcher trim between them, one piece in each of the two bays — 26 1/4" pieces on the 63" bench, 34 1/2" on the 96". Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of each stretcher on the face that will be hidden, then fasten with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Keep all three legs standing on the same flat surface as you work, and check that both stretchers land at the same height off the floor, or the finished bench will rock.

steps.title: Add the seat boards
steps.body: Lay the three 2x6 seat boards across the leg tops edge to edge; at 5 1/2" each they make a seat 16 1/2" wide. Center the middle leg under them and set the two end legs in from the ends by whatever overhang you want, the same amount at each end. Drill pocket holes up through the leg cap trim into the underside of the boards and fasten with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, so nothing shows on the seat surface. Work from the center board outward, pulling each board tight against its neighbour before you drive the screws.

steps.title: Add the bottom decorative support
steps.body: Center a bottom support low between the legs and fasten it with pocket screws and glue. On the 63" bench it is decoration; on the 96" bench add cross braces running from that center support out to each end leg, because over eight feet a bench with nothing tying the legs together low down will rack when someone slides along it. The plan gives no cut length for the bottom support or the braces, so cut them to the leg spacing you have actually built rather than to a number.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every face and round over the seat edges and the sharp mitered points of the X pieces, which sit at shin height. Fill any gaps at the miters and sand them flush before finishing. Then apply primer and paint, or stain and poly — worth knowing before you choose: paint hides a small miter gap far better than stain does.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Fancy X farmhouse bench with decorative X-braced legs
